content:
5 min read
Just now

--

The last decade belonged to NVIDIA. GPUs with separate memory pools became the default substrate for training and deploying deep learning models. If you wanted speed, you bought discrete VRAM.
Apple silicon changed the equation. The M1, M2, M3, M4 and now M5 chips introduced a unified memory architecture one pool accessible to CPU, GPU, and (in newer chips) the Neural Engine without transfers across a bus. But unified memory only matters if your framework understands it. MLX is an array framework for machine learning on Apple silicon, brought to you by Apple machine learning research, and it’s built from the ground up to exploit that architectural advantage.
Why This Matters: Real Numbers
Ollama just shipped MLX support. Here’s what actually changed when running Qwen 35B on an M5 MacBook Pro:
These are production numbers on a shipping MacBook. Decode performance (what bounds interactive chat) nearly doubled.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Testing was conducted on March 29, 2026, using Alibaba’s Qwen3.5–35B-A3B model quantized to NVFP4 and Ollama’s previous implementation quantized to Q4_K_M using Ollama 0.18. Ollama 0.19 will see even higher performance (1851 token/s prefill and 134 token/s decode when running with int4 quantization).Why the jump? Apple’s M5 has dedicated Neural Accelerators for matrix operations. MLX was built to use them. On older M4 chips, you still see 19–27% speedups across models ranging from 1.7B to 30B parameters.
The memory efficiency is equally important. A 30B mixture-of-experts model at 4-bit quantization uses 17 GB. A 14B dense model in native precision uses 9 GB. Both fit in a MacBook Pro without external GPUs or cloud costs.
The comparison against PyTorch MPS tells the story: MLX runs 25–30x faster for LLM inference on the same hardware. PyTorch MPS adapts CUDA semantics to Apple silicon. MLX was designed for unified memory from scratch.
The Core Design
The Python API closely follows NumPy with a few exceptions. MLX also has a fully featured C++ API which closely follows the Python API. If you’ve worked with NumPy or JAX, MLX’s API is familiar. Operations have the same names and signatures. The idea: minimize friction for researchers moving from desktop-oriented frameworks.
But MLX adds three critical features beyond NumPy’s basic array operations.

• Unified Memory: A notable difference from MLX and other frameworks is the unified memory model. Arrays in MLX live in shared memory. Operations on MLX arrays can be performed on any of the supported device types without transferring data.
2. Lazy Evaluation: Lazy evaluation lets us record a compute graph without actually doing any computations. This is useful for function transformations like grad() and vmap() and graph optimizations. When you write operations on MLX arrays, they aren’t executed immediately. The framework builds a computation graph and defers execution until you call mx.eval() or access a result.
3. Composable Function Transformations: Function transformations are transformations which take functions as input and return new functions as output. MLX has several function transformations. They typically fall into one of two categories: transformations for automatic differentiation and transformations to optimize the compute graph. mx.grad computes gradients. mx.vmap vectorizes over arrays. mx.compile JIT-compiles pure functions. These compose without special handling.
The Practical Edge
On-Device Inference: MLX enables efficient machine learning tasks and allows you to run large language models directly on device using Python and Swift. A 7B or 13B parameter LLM quantized to 4-bit precision fits in 4–8 GB of memory.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Research on Your Laptop: While high-performance hardware remains the best choice for large-scale deep learning tasks, MLX provides an alternative for running smaller experiments or prototyping directly on a Mac, which can be useful when a deep learning machine is not readily available. Training a Transformer on MNIST takes seconds. Experimenting with LoRA fine-tuning works without renting cloud compute.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Latency-Critical Workflows: MLX works with all Apple silicon systems, and with the latest macOS beta release, it now takes advantage of the Neural Accelerators in the new M5 chip, introduced in the new 14-inch MacBook Pro. The Neural Accelerators provide dedicated matrix-multiplication operations, which are critical for many machine learning workloads, and enable even faster model inference experiences on Apple silicon.
The Ecosystem
MLX is not monolithic. Higher-level packages wrap the core framework.

• mlx.nn: PyTorch-style module definitions. Familiar layer APIs let you build models without wrestling with low-level transformations.

• mlx.optimizers: Gradient descent, Adam, RMSprop. Standard optimizers, tuned for the unified memory model.

• mlx-lm: Text generation and fine-tuning. Run quantized LLaMA, Mistral, or Phi models. Fine-tune with LoRA adapters that you ship separately from the base model weights.

• mlx-whisper: Speech transcription with OpenAI’s Whisper models.

• MLX Swift: Embed the framework directly in iOS/macOS apps for on-device inference without network calls.
This ecosystem is still young. PyTorch’s ecosystem is orders of magnitude larger. But for the specific use case inference and light fine-tuning on Apple silicon MLX now offers native, idiomatic support that PyTorch’s adapted Metal backend cannot match.
The Trade-offs
MLX is not a replacement for PyTorch or JAX for datacenter work. It only runs on Apple silicon. If your team trains models on NVIDIA GPUs and deploys on a mix of cloud instances, switching to MLX doesn’t make sense.
But if your workflow looks like this:

• Prototype on a MacBook Pro.

• Fine-tune open-weight models with your data.

• Deploy to millions of iPhones via on-device inference.
Then MLX moves from curiosity to necessity. You get a framework that understands the hardware you’re deploying on, eliminates data movement overhead, and provides composable transformations for differentiation and optimization.
MLX is designed by machine learning researchers for machine learning researchers. The framework is intended to be user-friendly, but still efficient to train and deploy models. The design of the framework itself is also conceptually simple. We intend to make it easy for researchers to extend and improve MLX with the goal of quickly exploring new ideas.
Further Reading
